sentences.text: Well, as far as I know, he was a very-- the kindest man you could imagine.
He must have been a religious-- Orthodox Jew, before their war, or traditional-- you know, traditional probably more than ever.
But he was a very famous Viennese surgeon.
And he told me a few times that he's got a son who is in hiding.
So I don't know if he meant in Vienna or somewhere else.
He would have been probably in his early 40s-- something like this-- or maybe later.
Just because to me he was already old, you know?
Did you know his first name?
No, I didn't, no.
I just remember Dr.
Deutsch.
I just know Dr.
Deutsch.
I don't think we were [INAUDIBLE] sort of-- you know, we, we even wanted to know.
Not really.
So I just know that he was called, and I just know that I was taken to that little room where we slept.
There was-- they make, like, a make-- like a makeshift-- and I can see it.
Like two planks.
And then they put an oblong piece of wood on top of it, because we didn't have a table in this little room.
And this is where, where he laid me down, where he put me down.
He had only-- the only thing he had, it was a pocket knife.
That's all he had.
It was a pocket knife.
And he just-- he said that this item-- he said that he needs to make an incision.
He doesn't really know what it is, but he needs to make an incision.
So he, he made that incision, and it was on a Friday.
And I can see it.
He was standing in front of me, and suddenly, you know, from my body, all of this-- whatever was cruising in my body, all of this-- it just hit him-- just it went like a fountain, all over him.
And he said something, you know-- he uttered a word.
And you can imagine it was an operation, so there was no-- nothing, you know.
And I was in horrible pain.
And I was in shock, probably.
We didn't know then about shock.
I was in pain.
Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was unconscious.
I was drifted in and out of consciousness.
I just remember I was told, the only thing that relieved this pain-- it was at night.
As-- I was like this for about-- for the next week.
Bronka, B-R-O-N-K-A.
Weinstien-- I don't know how-- one second.
W-E-I-N-S-T-I-E-N, probably.
Weinstien.
That's how she-- or she use the S, but I think was Weinstien-- something like that.
But pronunciation is 'Bronka Weinstein.' She was the oldest.
She was about 20, perhaps, you know.
She already had had matric.
She was the only one that had it, really.
And she-- The only thing that helped is to-- being in hot water.
So, at night, she had a-- like, a little, um-- like a dish, a large dish.
And where we washed ourselves, we had this one.
And this is where she put me down.
And this was the only relief.
And she was bringing the hot water from somewhere at night, or somebody was bringing it for her.
I don't know.
But throughout those seven days, there were the roll calls.
And they hid me.

Those girls-- I was under the bed, again, or behind-- there was, like, I remember, like, a window with something there with a very old-- not a blind, or was it something-- torn blind and a curtain-- something like an old, colorful curtain.
I was hidden, or they put me there, covered, or whatever.
They hoped for the best.
I don't know even if they thought what they were going to do.
But the thing is that the roll calls were taking place, and the, the work had to be done.
Because we did-- we did this-- you know, the, the clothing that was coming-- there was coming clothing from Dutch Jews-- very exclusive clothes.
We had to select-- we were going through-- we were separating certain things from certain items.
Because we-- because-- how the-- one told the other, so we assumed.
We were told that this is-- because it was silk.
The underwear were many silk.
And apparently somebody there who could read Dutch, who could read-- and they read it, apparently, on the labels or something.
Somebody there, because I wasn't by myself.
And so we had to do whatever-- they had to do whatever presented itself.
Like, to clean the German quarters, like, to do, do the washing for the men-- whatever it was.
All of this had to be done.
Whilst I was hidden.
Don't ask me how they did it.
But this was not it yet.
But each time, a few men I know were involved in it.
But you see, life in camp was such-- it was going at such a pace that you really didn't have time to stop and to, you know, to really think or to really know how and what and when.
The, the, the end result was that they saved-- those girls, those remarkable girls-- they were heroines.
They were heroes, really.
And somehow, between them, each time somebody else-- when my name was called, somebody else answered for me.
Now I leave to your imagination what would happen if they would have been caught-- that they tried to defy because the German-- because this is what we were told.
You have to look at this body hanging there.
Because this is-- if you do anything out of place-- and he only escaped for a few-- he only stayed behind for a few hours.
So, you know, if they would have-- they would have realized how they did it, they probably-- they-- Whoever was going to answer for me stood behind.
Because if there was a roll call, you didn't have your normal place.
It wasn't a parade.
So you just ran out.
Wherever-- you know, it was a certain spot.
So wherever you stood that was in the middle of this, like, yard, surrounded by those dreary, drab places.
So in the middle, this is where it was.
So wherever you happened, they called, you ran out.
And whatever-- you were once in the front, once in the back, once in the middle-- whatever.
And you really could not distinguish that much between those girls-- between the boys, too.
You asked me this, Reuben.
You know how-- because I told you this already.
In the interview?
And I said-- you ask me how they knew the girls and boys-- how could they say?
OK?
How they wouldn't know if there would be somebody else-- was it a girl or was it a boy?
You couldn't really-- their hair was so shortly cropped, you couldn't really necessarily know.
If they stood there, they didn't want to look at you.
They didn't want-- as long as you were there, you answered, they couldn't care less, you know?
How many men were in the camp?
Yes, yes.
Yes.
And so this week went past.
And so I just am describing the rest of the stay in the camp.
Not that it was unremarkable, it is just that it happened, you know, at such a pace.
And people were coming.
People were leaving.
Then this camp was closed up or liquidated.
And this is when we went to the second camp-- to the Seibersdorf.
Well, I think approximately probably about 14 months-- close to 14 months-- something like this.
And then-- maybe 13 months.
And then I went to the next camp, which was already, you know, a camp that people were coming from everywhere.
So it was not a [INAUDIBLE], a [INAUDIBLE] camp.
But for a while I was in that camp.
Yes, there were-- no.
There were always around 100-- in the beginning, there were 150 people.
And then, let's say, not everybody survived.
I mean, some people-- because through the beatings, or because through the-- through the frost, or because through the lack of-- you know, they just got sick, and they just disappeared.
So the work what was done-- what needs to-- needed to be done-- it required around 150 men.
And the seven girls-- the girls were never replaced.
Because somehow-- I really don't know why-- perhaps the girls really didn't go outside to work.
You know?
Let's say-- occasionally, like I told you, on that Friday when I got sick, that I-- that we were doing it outside.
Otherwise, whatever presented itself, the seven girls did.
If it was the washing, if it was the carrying-- whatever it was.
But it was not really--
They were working on the road--
Yes.
For the men was on the road and unprotected.
And, because of the weather-- you know, sometimes, after all, they were hungry all the time.
As a matter of fact, we had some Dutch people.
The last part was-- the last couple of months, we had some Dutch, Dutch inmates-- Dutch prisoners coming.
Now I remember this.
And, in the beginning, they could not touch-- they said, this is food?
We don't-- we'd rather starve.
We don't want to touch it.
But we were already-- our bodies were already apparently partly used to it, or really craved-- any little bit that-- even a drink.
That was a time when a drink of water-- you went to have a bit of water, you felt full.
But they didn't want to touch it.
But a few days later, there were some rubbish bins, you know, where-- they were going through the rubbish bins and looking for-- and they didn't survive.
They did not survive.
They could not survive.
They could not survive this.
Well, they died, died of infections-- starve-- they couldn't-- the food that was there, it only very little offered.
It was barely to keep you alive.
Now, you see, for them it was not enough.
Or they did not--
We went gradually, you see?
We were sort of, you know-- gradually, this was less, this was less.
Or even-- if what happened in the beginning, somehow the family provided certain things where it made it easier for you to accept it.
They went suddenly-- they went-- when they had really a lot-- or-- in comparison to us-- they had-- and I just wanted to say for once-- one side.
They had really, you know, a lot, in comparison to us.
And when they came to the camp, and they saw, I mean, what it was there, it was unacceptable to them, you know.
They still perhaps had this-- there is a sense of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aesthetic.
This is what I'm trying to think.
You know, their aesthetic sense could not accept it temporarily.
But after a while, you wouldn't think that they're human beings.
After a while, they were uncontrollable.
Because it went straight away from one extreme to the other extreme.
Seven.
I remember, yes, Rozka Najman--
Yes.
Rozka, R-O-Z-K-A.
Najman, N-A-J-M-A-N.
Bronka Weinstien you have.
Rozka.
You have me or I in there.
Then it was Ewa Monstein.
Ewa, who lives in Los Angeles.
Then it was, um--
E-W-A-- No.
I didn't pronounce it right.
Ewa Monszein.
Eve, like Eve-- Monszein.
M-O-N-S-Z-E-I-N.
Monszein.
Or maybe J.
Maybe she pronounced a J at the end.
I know one-- the rest I don't remem-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] um-- Zlata was another one.
I don't remember her surname.
I know it was Zlata.
So how many is that?
Five.
Five, with Zlata.
Berta--
Berta-- I don't-- I can't remember now.
So how was there, six?
Without me, six.
With you, six. me, six.
And Bronka Weinstien you have.
Well, there was seven, so I just can't remember one.
Yes, Rozka-- Rozka.
We've got Rozka.
Like this Rozka.
No, you don't have Rozka.
No, you have Rutka.
Yes.
Uh-- Rozka-- no, no I don't.
No.
No.
Rozka.
She was from Bedzin.
And she was-- I didn't see her after the war at all.
I don't-- no, I didn't see her even in Peterswaldau.
I don't know.
A couple of them were taken away.
Well, they were unprotect-- the weather.
You know, we, we were still-- we sort of had an occasional shelter.
We worked inside, and we worked outside.
They went outside for the whole day.
Yes, people were coming into the camp-- like those Dutch people were coming into the camp.
Again, you see, I mean-- when I talk about it, I, I can visualize-- I can literally taste this-- we were disoriented at times.
We did not look.
We didn't know the next second.
So, in retrospect, I can see it, sort of, you know, in a neater way.
And I can remember it-- I can remember it visually.
I can remember it.
I can visualize it.
But at the time, I didn't really know how much I'm taking in or how much I am not.
I am only taking-- telling you about this what I took in.
The rest what happened, I don't really know.
So you spent, about, as you said, about a year in Oderberg.
And then you said the camp was liquidated.
What happened, and how did you get--
We went into-- we just-- we were just rounded up.
We had whatever couple of things, and we were taken.
The men and the girls were taken to Seibersdorf.
Yes?
S-E-I-B-E-R-S-D-O-R-F.
Yes.
And this is when we already then-- the seven girls kept in touch, but we were already-- there was an influx, you know, from other camps.
And we went to work there.
We went to some little factory or whatever.
I can't-- whatever presented itself.
We had to work from the morning till the evening.
So-- or they took us out, or we went in the kitchen, or whatever.
Because there were-- there were-- people were coming in.
They were sending them to other camps.
You see?
The idea was, I think, if possible to keep in those camps six weeks.
Something like six weeks, two months, you know?
So, from there-- Was Seibersdorf a bigger camp than Oderberg?
Much bigger, of course.
Because, you see, in Oderberg we already stayed for a long time.
In Seibersdorf, people were-- it was not a [INAUDIBLE].
It wasn't [INAUDIBLE] camp, it was a camp.
You see, you got up in the morning.
It took a long time that you-- till you could get to the water, till you could go to the makeshift toilets, till-- whatever.
Everything took a long time.
And then it had to be everything orderly.
So when they marched out, they marched out to work, sometimes it took a long time, all of this.
So if you don't know-- this is not like living in a home and you map up-- map out your day, and you know what you're going to do.
You know, here, we didn't know if we're going to stay for a year or if we-- we didn't know.
We just-- you lived from second to second.
So what did you do in the camp, for the period you were there?
Well, this is what I did, whatever presented itself.
Was it the washing, was it the cooking, was it the cleaning out?
Was it sometimes out-- or something.
And then you stood in the roll call, tzelappell.
Was it to watch the occasional beatings?
All kinds of things were happening.
It would tell me a couple of days, if I would-- tell-- if I could tell in the minutest detail.
It was just that something was all the time happening.
And throughout this all, it was those-- this-- those-- those-- we had-- By that stage, we had a lot of female guards.
And they were vicious.
They were atrocious.
They were just-- you know, it was just unbelievable.
I mean, there was no-- if the men-- if the men had no-- if the men were-- didn't have any compassion or didn't have, you know, any-- but the girls were even worse.
I mean, they were just absolutely, you know, like-- woo!
You know?
To sort of-- to, to sort of-- you know?
To really to devastate you.
To totally finish you off.
To sort of know-- You didn't register.
You were just nothing.
You were just absolutely nothing to them, you know?
To the men and to the women.
But it manifested itself-- perhaps because I was a girl, you know?
And it did-- it was-- And yet, throughout this all, what sustained us-- obviously, this would not have sustained us-- it-- what sustained us, it was the, the-- those seven girls, in particular.
It was-- this was already bonded.
You see?
And this is, you know, even a sort of-- behind the back, you know-- a way for even, you know, [INAUDIBLE] or something.
Because we really couldn't talk to one another during the day.
We could not talk to one another during the day.
But occasionally, as I said, we took a chance and we stole a glance or we-- you know, after all, you had eyes.
Occasionally you felt that it is-- you know, that you-- but you-- [SIGHS]
And some people who were older than I was, they said, if they will let me bleed, then I will survive.
I didn't consciously fight for my survival at any stage.
I was too young, apparently.
But I think that, in a passive way, or in a-- what-- what was my weapon?
It is that I somehow could respond to the kindness.
And there was a lot.
It's unbelievable that today you hear sometimes that people don't get on.
I don't really believe that.
I remember once that you stood in the queue for that little bit of water, for the little bit of bread.
When they called you out, you ran out and you stood wherever you were-- wherever was the line.
There was never any pushing or never any pulling.
And when we saw occasionally-- when we had this bit of a bread-- I just heard recently-- somebody who's my second cousin described how she had in her-- this mattress that supposedly to be a mattress.
I hid those photos that I showed you before.
And she hid her little potions of bread.
And they were molded.
She didn't eat them.
Because she felt that, if later on, suddenly she will have all this enormous amount of bread.
That was in her imagination.
That she will devour all of this bread.
And this will-- this will, you know-- this will let her survive.
So she didn't eat this bread, you see?
She hid it.
You know?
Like we saw the [INAUDIBLE]-- if we had-- sometimes, some people, when they got a bit of bread-- [GASP] you know?
A second later, it was nothing.
And the whole day, you know.
And some people waited with it for the last minute.
And very often those that waited, they could see that the other one is, you know, really-- you know, can't [INAUDIBLE].
So sometimes you gave them two bites of bread.

People were willing to do this for one another, to give up-- to give up your lifeline, to really, literally lay down your life, for, for even sometimes people that were-- we were all in the same boat, you know?
We didn't have any more or any less.
Although perhaps sometimes those girls that were in the kitchen perhaps occasionally-- they, they worked in the kitchen-- those large camps-- maybe occasionally they have.
They had a little bit more.
But, uh-- So this is how it was.
And yet, this is what allowed us to go a little bit longer.
Because somehow you could-- you saw that goodness.
You saw that caring.
They looked, you know-- sometimes the face was like-- had no expression, no emotion.
But sometimes a flick of a wrist, or sometimes a blink of an eye,  you know?
You know?
It's-- You know, I went-- it reminds me--
What reminded me of it.
I had to go for a checkup.
And I went to a doctor for a-- for a checkup that I am supposed to go--
I'm talking about myself, now.
I had to go for a checkup.
And that was at-- the doctor was a total stranger.
I've never met this doctor.
And he asked me, do you have certain-- does this happen or that happen?
I said no.
And he said, oh, thank god.
He never-- he never-- you know, it reminded me of this-- that he is a young man who doesn't know me at all.
Doesn't know me from Adam, you know?
And I come, and he could have been sort of business-like, you know, and just ask me what a doctor-- But no, you know, he was one of those that showed me the humane side of him, you know?
And this is how it was.
And this reminded me of it, you see?
You really didn't, didn't-- couldn't have conversations, at that stage.
You couldn't talk.
But sometimes, you know, a nod, a smile.
You know, this is-- this was just as important-- although without food, obviously you couldn't survive.
But this was just as important.
Oh, look, close about three months.
The camp was not liquidated, no.
It was not liquidated.
It's just that we were sent-- a group of us was sent to Blechhammer.
Blechhammer?
Well, we were sent by train.
We were sent-- we always-- we were sent-- it was a special wagon, you know?
We assumed that it was-- we don't know, you know?
They, they rounded us up.
We didn't have-- you know?
They just took us.
And they put us there.
They didn't tell us.
We didn't know where we are going.
Well, I think it would have been a couple of hundred, probably.
Five, I think.
Two of them were taken somewhere else, and five of them were taken to Blechhammer.
Five of them.
No.
No.
Because, you know, we went-- there were men, and there were girls, so, no.
In that particular-- it didn't take all of this-- no, I don't remember seeing them during-- between-- between Seibersdorf and Blechhammer, I don't remember seeing them.
Well, it's very hard to, to, to really be time-wise correct.
Because, again, you know, the-- there were-- the trains were very slow, and they stopped occasionally.
And occasionally you had to open it and a body fell out.
You know, all kinds of things were happening.
Again, we were like sardines, you know.
There were-- it was very small.
There were-- this was the group from Blechhammer.
It met-- this whole train was going to Blechhammer.
But, you see, there were other from other camps, as well.
So it was hard, really, to know who and how and when.
And, by that stage, you really didn't care.
I don't really remember, no.
No.
I think we were-- we were stunned.
I think-- I don't remember.
I didn't see anybody familiar.
I didn't know where I was going.
I was breathing in and breathing out.
Whatever there was, whatever stench there was.
That's all I remember.
I leave it to your imagination.
OK?
I think it was dark.
So it's the nighttime.
[SIGHS] There again, we, we stood in a-- we went in.
Was two or three.
We were marched-- we were marched to the camp, to Blechhammer.
It was a very-- well, it-- not-- just to walk.
And it was always-- the oldest were always schnell machen-- quick, quick, quick.
You know, today sometimes if I do certain things and I rush, and suddenly I stop myself, I say, what for?
I don't have a German behind me.
Why do I rush so much?
I'll manage.
And here you had to rush.
Schnell machen.
Schnell-- you couldn't stop, you couldn't stand, you couldn't-- you know, you couldn't do it!
It-- everything was quick, quick, schnell, schnell machen.
Verfluchte Juden, schnell machen.
'You verfluchte Juden, schnell machen, schnell, schnell.'
[SIGHS] The same what I said before.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well-- [SIGHS] you know, that you-- they wish on you to get the worst illnesses there was.
Yes?
Whatever there was then, that we already-- they, they-- plague, that's right.
That's right, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Not one, but whatever there was.
You know, like they used to have the seven [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
Pesach?
So this is-- this is what we were greeted with.
And this is what we heard from morning till night.
So we really-- we, we, we were in no mood, really, to, to pause and think.
So you-- whatever apparently you absorbed.
I didn't think really to tell you honestly-- I would not have thought then how much I have absorbed.
I wouldn't have thought how much I have absorbed, really.
Because you couldn't look to the left.
You couldn't look to the right.
You couldn't-- they didn't want you to see where you were.
We just were deposited there, to say it mildly, in Blechhammer.
And there was a vast camp.
There was a vast camp.
It was huge.
It was huge.
People from everywhere.
